. . Paris, the capital of France, is one of the main European cities and the world center of culture, art, fashion and gastronomy. The central part of the city, built in the 19th century, has wide boulevards and the Seine River. The most famous sights of Paris are the Eiffel Tower and Notre Dame Cathedral in the Gothic style, built in the 12th century. The city is also famous for its numerous cafes and boutiques with clothes from famous fashion designers on the rue Faubourg Saint-Honoré.

In the 1970s, Paris was known as a transitional, less-touristy city defined by intense nightlife (Le Palace), chic fashion, and, in some areas, a grittier, soot-stained atmosphere before massive cleaning efforts. It was a hub for intellectual, avant-garde art, and hedonistic partying among the elite, while the streets were heavily adapted for cars, sacrificing sidewalks for, in some cases, easier, faster transit.
